abilities: Petrifying Gaze: Anyone who looks directly at her face turns instantly to stone
Serpent Hair: Living, venomous snakes that writhe and strike independently
Flight: Golden wings enable swift aerial movement
Arrow-like feathers that can be launched as projectiles

weaknesses: Mirrors: Cannot see her own reflection without petrifying herself
Indirect Vision: Can be fought by reflecting her gaze or using periphery
Mortal Body: Despite her powers, her physical form can be wounded
Emotional Vulnerability: Attachment can cloud her judgment
habitat: Solitary caves and temples, places of desolation
behavior: Victim turned protector, vengeful yet sympathetic, isolation-driven

keyMyths.title: Heracles' Sixth Labor
keyMyths.description: As his sixth labor, Heracles was tasked by King Eurystheus to rid Lake Stymphalus of the Stymphalian Birds. The sheer number and dangerous nature of the birds made this a perilous task, requiring cunning and divine intervention.

keyMyths.title: Athena's Assistance
keyMyths.description: Unable to approach the birds directly due to the swampy terrain, Heracles received bronze clappers or a rattle from Athena. The noise startled the birds, causing them to take flight and become easier targets.

keyMyths.title: Killing the Birds
keyMyths.description: Once the birds were airborne, Heracles shot them down with his arrows. Some accounts suggest he used poisoned arrows to ensure their demise, while others describe him using a sling.

keyMyths.title: Migration or Extermination
keyMyths.description: While most accounts focus on Heracles killing the birds, some versions suggest that he only drove them away from Lake Stymphalus. These birds then supposedly migrated to other locations, such as the island of Ares in the Black Sea.

keyMyths.title: Symbolism of the Birds
keyMyths.description: Beyond the literal interpretation, the Stymphalian Birds are often seen as symbolic of various threats, including plague, pollution, or general chaos. Heracles' victory represents overcoming these challenges and restoring order.
keyMyths.source: Fontenrose, Joseph Eddy. Python: A Study of Delphic Myth and Its Origins.
